texts.orig: Abstract. Adhesive and quasiadhesive categories provide a general framework for the study of algebraic graph rewriting systems. In a quasiadhesive category any two regular subobjects have a join which is again a regular subobject. Vice versa, if regular monos are adhesive , then the existence of a regular join for any pair of regular subobjects entails quasiadhesivity. It is also known that (quasi)adhesive categories can be embedded in a Grothendieck topos via a functor preserving pullbacks and pushouts along (regular) monos.
texts.text: Abstract. Adhesive and quasiadhesive categories provide a general framework for the study of algebraic graph rewriting systems. In a quasiadhesive category any two regular subobjects have a join which is again a regular subobject. Vice versa, if regular monos are adhesive , then the existence of a regular join for any pair of regular subobjects entails quasiadhesivity. It is also known that (quasi)adhesive categories can be embedded in a Grothendieck topos via a functor preserving pullbacks and pushouts along (regular) monos.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In this paper we extend these results to M , N -adhesive categories , a concept which generalizes the notion of (quasi)adhesivity. We introduce the notion of N -adhesive morphism , which allows us to express M , N -adhesivity as a condition on the subobjects' posets. Moreover, N -adhesive morphisms allows us to show how an M , N -adhesive category can be embedded into a Grothendieck topos, preserving pullbacks and M , N -pushouts.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Since their introduction [LS05], adhesive and quasiadhesive categories have provided a powerful and elegant framework for studying and guaranteeing useful properties of algebraic graph rewriting systems [BHK22, CMR + 97, EEPT06]. Besides being useful for the study of rewriting systems, adhesivity and quasiadhesivity entail other properties of the underlying categories [JLS07, LS05]. In a quasiadhesive category any two regular subobjects (i.e. subobjects represented by a regular mono) have a join which is again a regular subobject. Vice versa, if regular monos are adhesive , then the existence of a regular join for any pair of regular subobjects entails quasiadhesivity [GL12]. Moreover, any (quasi)adhesive category can be embedded in a Grothendieck topos via a functor preserving pullbacks and pushouts along (regular) monomorphisms [GL12, Lac11], thus justifying the slogan that 'a category is (quasi)adhesive if pushouts of (regular) monomorphisms exist and interact with pullbacks as they do in a topos'.
texts.text: Since their introduction [LS05], adhesive and quasiadhesive categories have provided a powerful and elegant framework for studying and guaranteeing useful properties of algebraic graph rewriting systems [BHK22, CMR + 97, EEPT06]. Besides being useful for the study of rewriting systems, adhesivity and quasiadhesivity entail other properties of the underlying categories [JLS07, LS05]. In a quasiadhesive category any two regular subobjects (i.e. subobjects represented by a regular mono) have a join which is again a regular subobject. Vice versa, if regular monos are adhesive , then the existence of a regular join for any pair of regular subobjects entails quasiadhesivity [GL12]. Moreover, any (quasi)adhesive category can be embedded in a Grothendieck topos via a functor preserving pullbacks and pushouts along (regular) monomorphisms [GL12, Lac11], thus justifying the slogan that 'a category is (quasi)adhesive if pushouts of (regular) monomorphisms exist and interact with pullbacks as they do in a topos'.

texts.orig: To this end, in this paper we first establish a relationship between M , N -adhesivity and the existence of some binary suprema in the poset of subobjects of a given object. Generalizing the approach of [GL12], we introduce the notion of N -adhesive morphism ; then we show that, if M and N are nice enough, M , N -adhesivity entails the existence of suprema for some pairs of subobjects; vice versa, the existence of these suprema, together with every arrow in M being N -adhesive, is enough to guarantee M , N -adhesivity.
texts.text: To this end, in this paper we first establish a relationship between M , N -adhesivity and the existence of some binary suprema in the poset of subobjects of a given object. Generalizing the approach of [GL12], we introduce the notion of N -adhesive morphism ; then we show that, if M and N are nice enough, M , N -adhesivity entails the existence of suprema for some pairs of subobjects; vice versa, the existence of these suprema, together with every arrow in M being N -adhesive, is enough to guarantee M , N -adhesivity.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Moreover, the framework of N -adhesive morphisms allows us to generalize also the embedding results provided in [GL12, Lac11]. As in the (quasi)adhesive cases, and under some hypotheses on the classes M and N , an M , N -adhesive category admits a full and faithful functor into a Grothendieck topos, which preserves pullbacks and M , N -pushouts. Thus, this suggests the new slogan that 'a category is M , N -adhesive if M , N -pushouts exist and interact with pullbacks as they do in a topos'.
texts.text: Moreover, the framework of N -adhesive morphisms allows us to generalize also the embedding results provided in [GL12, Lac11]. As in the (quasi)adhesive cases, and under some hypotheses on the classes M and N , an M , N -adhesive category admits a full and faithful functor into a Grothendieck topos, which preserves pullbacks and M , N -pushouts. Thus, this suggests the new slogan that 'a category is M , N -adhesive if M , N -pushouts exist and interact with pullbacks as they do in a topos'.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Synopsis. In Section 2 we will recall the notion of Van Kampen square and the definition of M , N -adhesive category, showing how it relates with M -adhesivity and (quasi)adhesivity. Section 3 introduces the notion of N -(pre)adhesive morphism, which is used in Section 4 to provide a formula to compute certain suprema in the poset of subobjects of an M , N -category. Section 5 goes on the other direction: starting from the existence of certain suprema we can prove M , N -adhesivity. Using the results of these sections, in Section 6 we will show that, under some hypotheses on M and N , every M , N -adhesive category can be embedded in a Grothendieck topos via a functor preserving pullbacks and M , N -pushouts. Conclusions and suggestions for further work are in Section 7.

texts.orig: 6. An embedding theorem for M , N -adhesive categories
texts.text: 6. An embedding theorem for M , N -adhesive categories

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In this section we generalize the embedding theorem of [GL12, Lac11]: given a category X which is M , N -adhesive, if M and N are nice enough, we are able to construct a (Grothendieck) topos in which X embeds via a functor preserving pullbacks and M , N -pushouts.
texts.text: In this section we generalize the embedding theorem of [GL12, Lac11]: given a category X which is M , N -adhesive, if M and N are nice enough, we are able to construct a (Grothendieck) topos in which X embeds via a functor preserving pullbacks and M , N -pushouts.

texts.orig: Let us recall some terminology from basic sheaf theory (see, e.g., [Joh02b, MM12]). Let ( X , j ) be a site and { f i } i ∈ I be a covering family for X , i.e., f i : Y i → X and { f i } i ∈ I belongs to j ( X ). Given a functor X op → Set , a family { a i } i ∈ I of elements a i ∈ F ( Y i ) is compatible for F if whenever we have two arrows g : U → X i , h : U → X j such that f i ◦ g = f j ◦ h then F ( g )( a i ) = F ( h )( a j ). A functor F : X op → Set is a sheaf if for every covering family { f i } i ∈ I and compatible family { a i } i ∈ I there exists a unique amalgamation a ∈ F ( X ) such that, for every i ∈ I , F ( f i )( a ) = a i .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In this paper we have generalized three well-known results from the theory of (quasi)adhesive categories to the M , N -adhesive setting, adapting the techniques developed in [GL12].
texts.text: In this paper we have generalized three well-known results from the theory of (quasi)adhesive categories to the M , N -adhesive setting, adapting the techniques developed in [GL12].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: First, we have studied the poset of subobjects of an M , N -adhesive category. We have shown that given a mono in M and one in M∩N , then their supremum, called a M , N -union , exists and it is computed as the pushout of the pullbacks of the given monos.
texts.text: First, we have studied the poset of subobjects of an M , N -adhesive category. We have shown that given a mono in M and one in M∩N , then their supremum, called a M , N -union , exists and it is computed as the pushout of the pullbacks of the given monos.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Next, we have proved a kind of converse of the previous result: in presence of M , N -unions, we can guarantee M , N -adhesivity once we know that M is contained in the class of N -adhesive morphisms. This allows the reduction of the proof of the Van Kampen condition to the proof of stability of some square and of some pullbacks being pushouts. As an example, adhesivity of toposes can be readily proved using this methods.
texts.text: Next, we have proved a kind of converse of the previous result: in presence of M , N -unions, we can guarantee M , N -adhesivity once we know that M is contained in the class of N -adhesive morphisms. This allows the reduction of the proof of the Van Kampen condition to the proof of stability of some square and of some pullbacks being pushouts. As an example, adhesivity of toposes can be readily proved using this methods.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Finally, we have shown that, under some mild hypotheses about M and N , an M , N -adhesive category can be embedded in a Grothendieck topos via a functor preserving all the relevant structure (i.e. pullbacks and M , N -pushouts). Therefore, the slogan for which 'an adhesive category is one whose pushouts of monomorphisms exist and behave more or less as they do in a topos' holds even for M , N -adhesive categories.
texts.text: Finally, we have shown that, under some mild hypotheses about M and N , an M , N -adhesive category can be embedded in a Grothendieck topos via a functor preserving all the relevant structure (i.e. pullbacks and M , N -pushouts). Therefore, the slogan for which 'an adhesive category is one whose pushouts of monomorphisms exist and behave more or less as they do in a topos' holds even for M , N -adhesive categories.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: An open question is how to equip a category X with a preadhesive structure ( M , N ), suited to a given situation. One may notice that if a category is M , N -adhesive, then M must be contained in the class of N -adhesive morphisms. In particular, in the M -adhesive case, M must be a subclass of the class of adhesive morphisms. This suggests to investigate the poset of all preadhesive structures ( M , N ), with M contained in N a , in order to characterize the largest one, suited for the specific problem, for which X is M , N -adhesive.
